1: The fourth year of Jehoiakim — the year Nebuchadnezzar defeated Egypt at Carchemish (605 BCE), the turning point of the ancient Near East. This is the kairos moment when the word must be written down. Christ is the one in whom 'the time is fulfilled' (Mark 1:15) — the kairos of all kairos moments. The scroll that must be written in the year of geopolitical crisis is the prophetic counterpart to the incarnation: the word that must take form in the flesh at the precise fullness of time (Galatians 4:4).
2: 'Take a scroll and write in it all the words I have spoken to you ... from the day I first spoke to you in the days of Josiah until this day.' The comprehensive collection of the prophetic word — everything from the beginning gathered into one scroll. Jesus is the one who fulfills all the law and the prophets (Matthew 5:17): every word spoken by God from Moses through Malachi finds its unity in him. The scroll containing all previous divine speech is the type of Christ in whom all Scripture coheres (Luke 24:44-45: everything written about me in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms must be fulfilled).
3: 'Perhaps the house of Judah will hear of all the disaster I intend to bring on them, so that each person will turn from his evil way, and I will forgive their iniquity and their sin.' The purpose of the written word: repentance and forgiveness. 2 Peter 3:9: 'The Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance.' The divine desire for repentance and forgiveness runs from Jeremiah through the cross: Christ's death is God's ultimate 'perhaps' — his most urgent appeal for human response, written in blood rather than ink.
4: Baruch wrote on a scroll everything Jeremiah dictated — the human scribe faithfully recording the divine word. Jesus promised the Spirit who would guide the disciples into all truth (John 16:13) and bring to their remembrance all that Jesus said (John 14:26). The apostolic writings are the new covenant scroll: the Spirit's dictation through human messengers, as Jeremiah dictated through Baruch. The inscripturation of the word requires human partnership with divine speech.
5: 'I am banned; I cannot go to the house of the LORD.' Jeremiah confined — the prophet who cannot personally deliver the message. Christ is 'confined' in the deepest sense: 'I have a baptism to be baptized with, and how great is my distress until it is accomplished!' (Luke 12:50). The constraint is purposive — his confinement in the flesh, in the limits of human existence, is the very condition of his redemptive work. And the word goes out through others even when the prophet himself is prevented: the gospel travels through Spirit-filled witnesses even when its source has ascended.
6: 'You go and read from the scroll you wrote at my dictation — the words of the LORD — to all the people in the LORD's house on a day of fasting.' The word proclaimed publicly in the temple on a day of fasting — the fast that marks Israel's consciousness of need before God. Jesus reads from the scroll of Isaiah in the synagogue (Luke 4:16-21) on the Sabbath: the public proclamation of the word in the sacred space is the pattern of Baruch reading Jeremiah's scroll. But where Baruch's reading is received by officials who go to warn the king, Jesus's reading results in attempted murder (Luke 4:28-30) — the final rejection pattern being established.
7: 'Perhaps they will bring their petition before the LORD and each will turn from his evil way, for great is the anger and wrath of the LORD.' The urgent hope that the word will produce the repentance that averts wrath. Romans 10:14-17: 'How are they to believe in him of whom they have never heard? And how are they to hear without someone preaching? ... So faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ.' The written word read aloud is the means of repentance; Baruch's public reading is the OT type of the apostolic proclamation — the word heard, believed, repentance following.
8: Baruch did exactly as Jeremiah commanded. Faithful messengership — doing precisely what was entrusted, without addition or subtraction. Paul describes the apostolic calling in the same terms: 'This is how one should regard us, as servants of Christ and stewards of the mysteries of God. Moreover it is required of stewards that they be found faithful' (1 Corinthians 4:1-2). The faithfulness of the messenger is not about the messenger's greatness but about the integrity of the transmission — the word reaching its hearers unchanged.
9: A fast proclaimed in Jerusalem — the people gathered before God in acknowledged need. Christ himself fasted forty days and forty nights (Matthew 4:2) — entering the condition of human need and dependence before the Father. The fast is the physical expression of what the soul acknowledges before God: 'we have nothing apart from you.' Jesus teaches that true fasting is not performed for human approval but before the Father who sees in secret (Matthew 6:16-18). The fifth-year fast in Jerusalem is the historical moment when Baruch reads — the receptive moment of hunger before the word.
10: Baruch read the words in the LORD's house — the word proclaimed in the sacred space where God's name dwells. Jesus taught daily in the temple (Luke 21:37-38); 'all the people were hanging on his words.' The temple as the house of prayer (Matthew 21:13) is the proper place for the word of God to be proclaimed. But Jesus's body is the true temple (John 2:21), and from him the word goes out into all the world — the temple becomes a person, and the word proclaimed from the temple becomes the Word made flesh.
11: Micaiah heard all the words of the LORD from the scroll — the chain of hearing begins. The word of God always travels through those who hear and transmit: Jeremiah → Baruch → Micaiah → officials → the king. Romans 10:15-17 traces the same chain: preacher → word → hearing → faith. Each link in the chain is essential; the word that reaches Jehoiakim's court passes through multiple human hearers. The Incarnation is God himself entering the chain — no longer sending messengers but coming as the message.
12: The officials sat in the secretary's chamber — the center of political administration. The word of God reaches the halls of power: Elishama, Gemariah, Elnathan, and the other officials all hear. Jesus before the Sanhedrin, Paul before Felix and Festus and Agrippa (Acts 24-26), the apostles before the Jerusalem council — the word of the Lord consistently penetrates the chambers of power, and the powerful must decide what to do with it. The officials' response (sending for Baruch, hearing the scroll, being alarmed) is far better than the king's.
13: Micaiah reported to all the officials all the words he had heard. The faithful transmission of the word received — one who heard it brings it to others who have not yet heard. The chain of witness: 'What we have seen and heard we proclaim also to you' (1 John 1:3); 'We cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard' (Acts 4:20). The compulsion to report the word received is the pattern of all gospel witness — from Micaiah in the secretary's chamber to the apostles in the court.
14: The officials sent for Baruch — 'Take the scroll ... and come here.' The word is wanted, summoned. There are hearts in every generation who want to hear the word: Cornelius who sent for Peter (Acts 10:7-8), the Ethiopian eunuch reading Isaiah (Acts 8:28), Lydia whose heart the Lord opened (Acts 16:14). The officials' summoning of Baruch is the type of the spiritual hunger that draws out the messenger — the seeking that Christ promises will find (Matthew 7:7-8).
15: 'Sit down and read it to us.' The officials give the word an audience; they sit and listen. Mary sitting at Jesus's feet listening to his teaching (Luke 10:39) is the posture of the receptive hearer — what Jesus calls 'the good portion which will not be taken away from her.' Against Martha's distracted activity, the sitting and listening at the feet of the one who speaks the word is the essential act. The officials who say 'sit down and read' are, in that moment, doing what Mary did.
16: 'When they heard all the words, they turned to one another in alarm.' The word of God that provokes fear and alarm — Hebrews 4:12: 'The word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing to the division of soul and spirit, of joints and marrow, and discerning the thoughts and intentions of the heart.' The officials' alarm is the right response to a word that exposes the true situation. The word of Christ is not comfortable; it is confronting — and the question is whether the alarm produces repentance or rejection.
17: 'How did you write all these words? At his dictation?' The officials want to understand the mechanics of the word's origin. John 1:18: 'No one has ever seen God; the only God who is at the Father's side, he has made him known.' The question of how God's words come to human beings is answered definitively in the incarnation: not by a scribe taking dictation but by the Son who is himself the Word — the 'at his dictation' of all creation and all redemption. Jesus does not transmit a message; he is the message (John 14:6: I am the truth).
18: 'He dictated all these words to me, and I wrote them in ink on the scroll.' The ink-on-scroll recording of the divine word is the OT medium; the NT medium is the same word written on hearts (2 Corinthians 3:3: written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts). Baruch's ink becomes the Spirit's inscription; the external scroll becomes the internal transformation. The word that Baruch copied is the word that Christ writes in the believer through the Spirit.
19: 'Go and hide — you and Jeremiah — and let no one know where you are.' The officials protect the prophet and his scribe from the king's wrath. Matthew 2:13-15: the angel warns Joseph to take the child and flee to Egypt. The hidden prophet preserved from royal wrath is the type of the hidden Messiah preserved from Herod's sword. God hides his servants — 'He will hide me in his shelter in the day of trouble' (Psalms 27:5) — because the word's survival is necessary for the purposes of redemption.
20: The officials stored the scroll in Elishama's chamber and went to report to the king. The word entrusted to a keeper while the official proceedings continue — the gospel entrusted to believers while the world's systems carry on. The scroll in Elishama's chamber will survive the king's burning because it is not the only copy: the word of God cannot be finally destroyed, even when the visible record is consumed. 1 Peter 1:23-25: 'The word of the Lord remains forever. And this word is the good news that was preached to you.'
21: The king sent Jehudi to get the scroll — the word brought into the presence of royal power. The same dynamic occurs at the passion: Jesus is brought before the powers of empire and religion. Every power in the world eventually must face the word of God — not as an abstraction but as a specific confrontation. Jehudi reading the scroll to Jehoiakim is the type of the word confronting power: the only question is the response.
22: Jehoiakim in his winter house, a fire burning in the fire pot. The comfortable warmth of the winter house — physical comfort that insulates from the word's demand. Jesus's parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21) captures the same insulation: stored-up goods, bodily ease, 'eat, drink, and be merry.' The fire that should burn in the heart (Luke 24:32: 'Did not our hearts burn within us while he talked to us on the road?') burns instead in the fire pot — physical warmth that replaces spiritual reception.
23: Each time Jehudi read three or four columns, the king cut off the section and threw it into the fire — until the entire scroll was consumed. The systematic destruction of the written word of God: Jehoiakim's knife cutting the columns is the type of every attempt to suppress, destroy, or silence the gospel. The burning of the scroll is also the type of the crucifixion: the Word made flesh is crucified. Isaiah 40:8: 'The grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our God will stand forever.' The burned scroll is rewritten; the crucified Word is raised. The cross is Jehoiakim's knife on the ultimate scale — and the resurrection is the re-dictated scroll.
24: 'Neither the king nor any of his servants who heard all these words was afraid, nor did they tear their garments.' The complete absence of the response the word demands — no fear, no repentance, no torn garments (the sign of mourning and contrition). Acts 7:57-58: when Stephen delivered his prophetic speech, 'they stopped their ears and rushed together at him and cast him out of the city and stoned him.' The hardened heart that meets the word with violence rather than repentance is the consistent pattern: Jehoiakim → the Sanhedrin → all who reject the crucified Christ.
25: Elnathan and Delaiah and Gemariah pleaded with the king not to burn the scroll — he would not listen. There is always a remnant who attempt to preserve the word, who intercede for its reception. The 7,000 who have not bowed the knee to Baal (1 Kings 19:18; Romans 11:4-5) are the minority who carry the word through periods of official rejection. The officials who plead with Jehoiakim are the type of the remnant by grace — those in whom the divine mercy has produced a different hearing, preserved through the faithfulness of God.
26: 'The king commanded ... to seize Baruch the scribe and Jeremiah the prophet. But the LORD hid them.' God's protection of his servants from the wrath of human power. The resurrection is the ultimate 'but the LORD hid them': human power kills the Word made flesh, but God does not leave him in the tomb. Acts 2:24: 'God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it.' The scroll is burned but the scribes are hidden; Christ is crucified but raised — the word of God outlasts every attempt to silence it.
27: 'After the king had burned the scroll ... the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah.' The burning does not silence the word — it comes again, immediately. John 1:5: 'The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.' The word that was burned (type: crucified) comes again (type: risen). The resurrection is not a new message but the same message, vindicated and re-given: 'He is risen' is the re-dictation of the burned scroll. The word that Jehoiakim tried to destroy is immediately reconstituted in the divine speech.
28: 'Take another scroll and write on it all the same words that were in the first scroll, the one Jehoiakim burned.' The command to re-inscribe the destroyed word — the resurrection pattern. 1 Peter 1:23-25: 'You have been born again, not of perishable seed but of imperishable, through the living and abiding word of God ... The word of the Lord remains forever.' The word that cannot be destroyed is made permanent not by human preservation but by divine imperishability. The scroll is burned; the word endures; another scroll is taken — because the word's existence does not depend on the first copy.
29: 'Why have you written in it that the king of Babylon will certainly come and destroy this land?' Jehoiakim's objection to the word: it says uncomfortable things about power. The powers that crucify Christ object to the same thing: 'He said he was the Son of God' (John 19:7); 'We found this man misleading our nation' (Luke 23:2). The word of God that announces judgment on present powers is always the word that power seeks to silence — but the silence fails, because the judgment announced comes true.
30: 'He will have no one to sit on the throne of David.' Jehoiakim's dynastic pretension — the king who would silence God's word loses his own succession. The contrast with the Davidic promise in Jeremiah 33:17 is stark: David's line will never fail a man to sit on the throne — because that man is ultimately Christ. Those who set themselves against the word of God lose what they sought to preserve through that opposition; those who receive the word and the King it announces share in his eternal reign (Revelation 22:5: they will reign forever and ever).
31: 'I will punish him and his offspring and his servants for their iniquity.' The judgment falls not only on the individual but on those who participate in covenant-breaking. Yet 2 Corinthians 5:19-21 reverses this: 'In Christ God was reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them ... For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.' The punishment that should fall on Jehoiakim's offspring — on all who reject the word — has fallen on Christ, so that those who receive the word receive not punishment but righteousness.
32: 'Then Jeremiah took another scroll ... and wrote on it at Jeremiah's dictation all the words of the scroll that Jehoiakim had burned. And many similar words were added to them.' The re-dictated scroll is not a mere copy — it contains everything before plus more. The resurrection gospel is the same word as the prophetic word, but fuller: Hebrews 1:1-2 traces the movement from the partial prophetic speech to the definitive speech in the Son. The scroll with additions is the type of the full canon — the burned-and-rewritten word of God that is richer than what came before, with everything the prophets said now illuminated by the passion, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. The word Jehoiakim burned is the word that outlasts all empires, all fire pots, all scribe's knives — living and abiding forever (1 Peter 1:23).
